speaker: interviewer
text: You can go ahead and read that, please. December 2, 1994. Survivor Margot Heumann. The interviewer is Carolyn Richmond. The city is New York. Language is English. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your name and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. My name is Margot Heumann, which is pronounced 'human' in the United States, to non-- by non German-speaking people. And it's spelled M-A-R-G-O-T H-E-U-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on February 17, 1929, officially. However, my parents had to change my date to 1928, for some political reasons. But my birth certificate is 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the political reasons were?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had something to do with getting me into a school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. In what city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Hellenthal, which is a small village in Germany, in a region called Eifel. It's not too far from the Belgium border. And I lived there, I think for the first four or five years of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Eifel?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-I-F-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've lived with your family there for--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my, my parents and two years later, my sister was born. And we lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Carl, and my mother's name was Johanna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember their age, or when they were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born December 7, 1898. And my mother was born July 25, 1902.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister, what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Lore, L-O-R-E. And Lore was born on March 27, 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents do? Your father was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father owned a variety store. And we lived above the store. And my grand-- my maternal-- paternal grandfather lived across the street. And he had a huge house with a-- with barns and he dealt with in cattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hellenthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived there for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years, about. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in Hellenthal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember that much. I remember breaking-- the only time I ever broke my leg skiing was in Hellenthal, when I was four, four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started skiing then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started skiing then. It was-- it is now a ski resort, by the way, Hellenthal. I think I had a very happy childhood. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I spent a lot of time-- my grandmother-- I never knew my grandmother. But I spend a lot of time in my grandfather's house, where my father's younger brothers and sisters still lived. And I was in a great extended family. Hellenthal is in the mountains. We did a lot of hiking. That's really-- I don't remember that much about it otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- do you remember Jewish life in Hellenthal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know we-- there was a synagogue belonging-- that had two small communities. There was a community called Blumenthal nearby, and we shared a synagogue. And my, my mother kept a kosher house. I don't think we were-- I would say probably conservative, as far as Judaism goes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the town Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were about-- no. The whole town was maybe four-- it was a village-- maybe 4,000 or 5,000 people. And there were about 15 Jewish families in that town itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember at all what it was like to be Jewish in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have-- I have no-- I had no problems. I was not aware that I was different or anything. I just-- I had a normal childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where-- you left Hellenthal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Hellenthal because-- I don't remember the reasons. But my father had to sell the business. Now, this was be-- well, it was-- '29, it was about 1935. And I don't think actually it had anything to do with Hitler at that time. But he did have to sell. And he did get a job working for his brother, who had three stores in three cities, called Lippstadt, Soest, and Gutersloh, which is in Westphalia. And we moved to Lippstadt. And my father managed the store for him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of store?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was like a department store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Big?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big, very big. Quite big, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of stuff did they sell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. I would-- I would say it's similar to a Woolworth, but there was no food. You know, clothing and all kinds of merchandising and supplies, but no food at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in that city?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a beautiful house. And we had-- the River Lippe was flowing beyond our garden, and I learned to swim there. And I learned to swim-- I'll never forget this. They put a rope around me, they threw me in the river, and I learned to swim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a good life. I, I-- I had a very loving, supporting home life. I had-- it was great family life, very close. And there was no anti-Semitism at that time. I mean, I was Jewish. We belong to the temple. I went to Hebrew school, and I went to Sunday school. But I never remember anything, actually, until '38, at any time did anybody said anything to me about being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother never worked. My mom, my mother kept house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any other family members live in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just the four of us and a dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: A dog. Do you-- what was your dog's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Waldi.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's--do you remember the street name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a German Shepherd called Waldi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old-- your sister was two years younger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years younger than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we had to move again. I mean, now the Hitler era started coming in. But we-- I did start grade school in-- kindergarten, I believe it was, in Lippstadt. My sister never did, because we have to move to Bielefeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you moved, what was life like in Lippstadt. Was Jewish life--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, again, Lippstadt is-- maybe had at that time 150,000, 200,000 inhabitants. There was a temple. There were approximately-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a lot of Jewish friends. I would say there must have been at least 100 Jewish families. But I don't really remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of children to play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a lot of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember going on hikes every-- every with my family. I remember my father taking me in May to see lillies of the valley and to a mountain. And I can still smell them now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it smell like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just-- I don't know, I rode a bicycle, I went swimming, I went skiing, and I went to school. And we did have-- visited my grandparents a lot, my mother's parents who lived in Hochneukirch, which is near Cologne.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-O-C-H-N-E-U-K-I-R-C-H. It's also a small town. But we spent a lot of time there. And it was, it was a nice family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel to visit your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. And when I was-- as a matter of fact, when I was six years old, I was allowed to travel by myself. And I still can remember that. And I went by train to visit-- to stay for the summer with my grandparents. And my mother just packed sandwiches and food and she turned me over to the conductor. There was no problem. And my grandmother picked me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were summers like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather-- great. My other cousins were there. My grandfather had a cigar factory. And they had this huge house, with a garden that's like in a-- I don't-- I would say at least half an acre, and all cultivated. And we used to play in it and run around in it. And I-- we used to steal tobacco and make cigars and smoke cigars in the back of the garden. [LAUGHS] And my cousin was like six months-- this one cousin I played with all the time was, six-- he was six months older than I was. And this was my aunt's son. And as a matter of fact, they all survived the war, because she had been married Catholic. But they had to go into hiding. But my aunt and her five children did survive and stay in Germany in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a lot of family there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother had-- came from a family of five. And my father came from a family of 11. So I had a lot of cousins, a lot of uncles and aunts.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, they were all in Germany. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were holidays like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Great. I mean, everybody got together at my grandparents, either one or the other. And it was a really family feast. And as far as I know, my grandparents on both sides of my family had been in Germany since about the 13th century.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go-- come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: One came from France. And I think the other came from Spain at one time, the other part of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--did you have relatives that are still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It's-- no, actually as far as I know, everybody was in Germany at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the holidays like, the family feasts? What kind-- a lot of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all I remember my grandfather had his head shaved. Every Friday afternoon before the Sabbath, my grandma shaved his head. And every holiday before the holiday started, his head was shaved almost like a mirror, so I remember that. And everybody got together, and it was in the summer. We ate outdoors. My-- they had-- my grandparents were quite well off, so they didn't-- my grandmother didn't cook. They had help, and they did the cooking and they served us. And we all went together on hikes, and we went mountain climbing. And we just did-- played games. And we just did things together. It was like a great family thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their house like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was a three-story house. And the-- my-- actually, the living quarters, more or less, were downstairs. There was-- there were only two baths. One was downstairs and one was upstairs. And then the bedrooms were upstairs. And the attic has-- it had another two or three bedrooms. So it was like at 12- or 13-room house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your house like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lippstadt?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lippstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, great. That was beautiful. It was a villa. Really nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Big?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big, yeah, very big. We all had our-- my sister and I had our own room. We also had a game room. We had a finished-- a basement that was like a game area. And a beautiful garden, with the river behind it. Nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the game room?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played ping pong. And we had a dollhouse, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get along very well with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was heavy. And I used to call her-- it was in German what I called her. But translated it means that she was lazy, fat, and never moved, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you played a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my mother made me take her all the time, so I resented her, I mean, at that time. Because every time I went somewhere, I had to take her along. And I didn't want to take her along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met friends. And I had to take her on my bicycle. I used to use my father's bicycle and put her on the handlebar in the middle. And I always had to take her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I had children, my daughter never, ever had to watch her brother. Because I remembered. Not ever. Because I just didn't want her to feel the way I did, because she was the oldest too. What were your friends like back then?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Actually, the friends that I really remember are in Bielefeld. I don't remember any friends from Lippstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What friends do you remember from Bielefeld?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we moved to Bielefeld in [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] '37 or so. And then my father did work for a while with my-- in my uncle's business. I had another uncle in Bielefeld, a brother of my father's, and he had a store there. And he worked with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my uncle had to close up his businesses because of Hitler. And he moved to Holland, the uncle, you know, who owned the business that my father was working in at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was I think, '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how do things start to change with Hitler? What did you first notice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't really notice anything until Kristallnacht. But I just noticed that my father kept on losing his job and we couldn't-- couldn't find another job. And we had-- we couldn't afford to live where we had been living, because there was no money coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my lifestyle changed somewhat. But that's the only thing I noticed. I was not aware of any anti-Semitism-- What were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --until '38 I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were things like in school during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- then when we moved to Lipp-- to Bielefeld, my sister was in school then too. And my cousin-- this uncle that my father worked for-- she, her name is Lotte, and she was in school. And the three of us were the only Jewish children in this one school in Bielefeld. There were other Jews in Bielefeld. But it just happened that we were the only ones at that school, as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you treated differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not until-- there was no difference until '38. None at all. There were-- I never encountered any prejudice until Hit-- until Kristallnacht. I think that's what changed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when you had moved to Bielefeld?

speaker: interviewee
text: We then moved into a house which we had to share with another Jewish family, because, you know, things were beginning to change. And my father worked for an organization called [GERMAN] [GERMAN] [GERMAN] [GERMAN] which means like self help for Jewish-- for German Jews. And he was involved in getting help for people and also trying to get people out of Germany. I don't know why we didn't get out, but we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was a-- I don't really know. It was a business job. I mean, he worked in the-- in an-- with the organization, he was in the office. He had a secretary who also lived with us, who was also Jewish. And I think things-- that's when things first started to change and that I was aware of, that it wasn't so nifty to be a Jew in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still celebrating holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish holidays? Oh, yes. We always--we belonged to a temple in Bielefeld. Yes, I went to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many Jewish families in--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the most a hundred, at the very most, not that many. And Bielefeld at that time probably had about half a million people.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: M-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that city located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In West-- Westphalia. It's not too far from Hanover. Most people know Hanover better.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did things start to change then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, things really began to change with Kristallnacht. That's the first time that I really became aware of what will be. And my parents didn't really talk to us about it, because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. They didn't want-- I guess they didn't want to upset us or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why things were changing at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was told about it after Kristallnacht. Before that, no. Because there were-- I mean, we were the only Jewish family on the street. There was no prejudice. I played with all my friends. It was just when-- when I started wearing the Jewish star. That's when people became aware of it and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start wearing the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was about 30-- in '38, after, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember of Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my mother crying hysterically. My father was never taken to a concentration camp, because he worked for this organization. I remember my aunt coming over because my uncle had been taken to Buchenwald. This is this cousin I told you about that was in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is what year? This was in 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: '38. Right after they smashed everything in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know about it until the next day. No one told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother told me what happened. And that they had broken-- that the store was completely-- all the windows were smashed in in my uncle's store, that the temple was demolished, that the Germans didn't like Jews anymore. And I, I mean I kept saying, well, why? I mean, what have we done or something? You know, it's very hard to understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, well, she doesn't know anything we have done. But they just don't like this anymore. And I mean, how can I-- at the time, I was nine years old. So how can you explain that to-- or seven-- to a nine-year-old or a seven-year-old, really? What happened after that? What happened to the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: The store was then taken over. Oh, they had signs on it. Don't buy from Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They made it so that it was no longer profitable to keep the store. So my uncle was forced to sell it at a price that the Germans stipulated that he sell it for. And consequently, they left in '39 and came to the United States, my uncle, his wife, and their two children. And you stayed-- your family stayed in-- I don't think-- see, I, I was too young to realize it. But I don't know why we didn't get out. I think part of it was money. Because my aunt who died five years ago had given me a bunch of letters that my parents wrote to them, one is dated '42 yet. And it's pathetic, because all my father is asking, get us out, get us out, get us out. And I mean, I'm going to be very honest with you. I don't think my relatives did what they could have done to get us out. So I don't only blame the Germans, I blame the whole world now. Because, I mean, they, they could have done something, to raise money and get an affidavit to get people out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how were people getting out at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only way to get out. There was a quota system. We had a quota that would have been OK. But you did need an affidavit, and you needed money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they used-- and now, I had about eight sets of relatives that had gotten out of Germany. And all the eight together couldn't raise enough money to get one family out? I don't buy that anymore. Now, everybody is dead, so there's nothing-- I mean, I can't blame anyone anymore. But I just feel that they should have done more than they did. What happened when you were there? What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then, then things began to happen. I had to wear the Jewish star. I was-- well, first in '38, we were kicked out of school. And there were only the three of us-- my cousin, my sister, and I was standing outside crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because we don't know what was happening. And then we decided to walk home together. And then my parents-- since most of the Jewish teachers were also dismissed, they did set up some schools for us, with Jewish teachers. And we did attend these schools and some Hebrew school. And the temple was still there. But then we had to wear the Jewish star. And we weren't allowed to go swimming, I weren't allowed-- wasn't allowed to do anything. I wasn't allowed to go anywhere. My mother was afraid to let us go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did, I just took the star off. I had it on my outer coat. I just didn't wear the coat. And I just did-- and went swimming and did the things that I liked to do anyhow. I mean nobody-- my parents didn't know about this. They didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I didn't think of it. I just, well, why should all of a sudden give this up? But then the children, my friends began to change. And that was the end of that too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to be my friends any longer. The only friends I have left-- I had left were Jewish friends. And I had a very good friend called Barbel Gottschalk, and she was deported to Warsaw in 1940. And I remember going with her to the place where we were-- where they were gathering, and I was carrying her backpack. And I remember saying good-bye to her. And I-- we corresponded, and I did send her packages for a while. And then I understand she was killed there. And the reason we stayed in Germany as long as we did is because my father was working for this Jewish organization, and he also had won the Iron Cross fighting for Germany in World War I. And I don't know why, but they gave him some privileges for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in the Army for Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They all were. I had-- two of his brothers, two of my uncles who died, were killed in World War I, fighting for Germany. So they were-- but because of that, they, they gave us some privileges.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other privileges did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the main privilege was going to Theresienstadt rather than another-- than a concentration camp. And also we stayed in Germany until the end of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what went on after 1938, when you started to wear the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to wear the star. We didn't get food-- we didn't get--have enough food, because we didn't get, you know, the ration cards. My mother, we had chickens in the backyard, so we could have some eggs. And we had to make do of what we had. More and more people were deported to the various concentration camps. Fewer and fewer Jews were left. We didn't have a temple anymore. We didn't have a school anymore. My father got me a job, because he didn't want me to hang around just at home. At the time, I was maybe 11 or 12. And I got a job as a messenger for a German firm. They made ties, a tie manufacturer. And I delivered things, [BACKGROUND NOISE] just to keep me busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they pay you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they paid me. They even paid social security for me. I mean, this is amazing. And now I'm getting social security from Germany because of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Josten.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-O-S-T-E-N. Josten Company.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a year. And they were not Jewish. And they also-- you know, there was risk involved in hiring a Jewish kid. But they did it anyhow. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hire other Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was-- they had about one or two people working in the factory. But there were hardly any Jews left. Towards the end, we were three families left, four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where everybody was going when they were deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. We didn't know that there was-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't know that there really were concentration camps the way-- we thought they were going into a ghetto and just lived together in ghettos, as I-- you know, learned before about Polish ghettos and things like that. But we didn't think there were any extermination camps or anything like that. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had they told you about Polish ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean before Hitler? That Jews lived together in ghettos, because they wanted to live there, and it was just a way of Jewish life. There was self-rule, no abuse, no hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then I found out that they weren't really ghettos. I mean, the Warsaw ghetto was a ghetto. But it was really also an extermination.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people find out that were being deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were given notices that we had to gather at such and such a place, and that you could bring a suitcase or whatever your belongings were and that you had 24 hours and leave everything else at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those four years between 1938 and '42, you were--your father was still working?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father worked till the end, yes. We were the last-- there were three Jewish families that went on the last transport to Theresienstadt from Bielefeld. And the other Jewish family was a, a young-- a very young family with a little baby. And then other one was my father's secretary, who was really a single woman, who fell in love with a physician who had been married-- a Jewish man who had been married to a German. And he voluntarily went to Theresienstadt with us when we did go. And eventually, he had to lead a transport of children from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz. And she volunteered to go along as his nurse. And they were both-- they all ended up in the gas chamber. 500 children plus the doctor and this woman, Anneliese. And they just-- that was the end of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was that, that transport like in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was towards the end of the year. I don't know the exact date. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was it like? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were only the three families. We were put--we traveled in a regular railroad. It wasn't like any other travelers-- traveling after that. We-- we were allowed to take what we could carry. And somehow-- I really didn't mind going. Because I was sick and tired of staying where the-- I had no one to play with, I had no one there. I was-- I couldn't do anything anymore. And people used to point fingers at us, there go the Jews because we had to wear the stupid star. And it just-- I thought, gee, maybe I'll be together with other Jews. I mean, I was a child. I didn't know what-- we did not know what a concentration camp was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the people who were pointing fingers at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some I knew and some I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I felt terrible. Like some said, oh, she doesn't look like a Jew at all. Like you know, like the-- some people think Jews have horns or something like that. And then sometimes people were sympathetic, like, I mean, we were small children, [BACKGROUND NOISE] so. But it just-- it's just life changed so drastically. There was-- it was much better than life in the camps, but I didn't know that at the time. But it was just wasn't-- it was not so nice living there anymore. And people who were your friends two years ago became your arch enemies and didn't want to have anything to do with you anymore. And the ones that still would have wanted to be your friends were afraid to do so, because it would be at the risk of their own lives. And then I saw the Hitler youth marching. And I remember we had a parade because Hitler was supposed to come. And they sang this song in German. First of all, I thought it was exciting, until I heard the song, which is like [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means when the Jewish blood squirts out from the knife, everything is-- I mean, I just didn't want-- I didn't mind going.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
